nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally found in deep lagoons and seaward reefs at approximately 10–120 m depth in tropical waters between 30°N and 33°S

decorPreferences: live rock with numerous hiding places

diet.notes: The Mertensii Butterflyfish is an opportunistic omnivore. In the wild it has been documented feeding on benthic algae and small invertebrates such as zooplankton, small crustaceans, and polychaete worms. In captivity it generally accepts a variety of meaty foods — mysid shrimp, chopped marine seafoods, and quality frozen preparations — alongside marine algae. Small, frequent feedings offered several times daily are commonly advised, particularly during the initial acclimation period.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Totti; 2577x1933. Sharp, vibrant aquarium specimen showing the diagnostic crosshatched black-edged scales, bright yellow posterior wedge, white-and-yellow tail, and black eyebar; whole fish in frame.
Photo by Susan Prior; 1500x1500. In-situ wild individual, well-lit and in focus, clearly showing the pearlyscale crosshatch pattern, orange-yellow posterior and black eyebar.

Photo by Debra Baker (via iNaturalist/GBIF); 2048x1536. In-situ shot on a colorful reef, correct ID with visible crosshatch scaling and vibrant yellow posterior; a slightly softer alternate showing the fish in natural habitat.

summary: A Pacific reef butterflyfish with a white body crosshatched by black-edged scales and an orange-yellow posterior. Generally considered intermediate-care and not reef-safe; best suited to large fish-only or FOWLR systems with ample swimming space.
careNotes:
The Mertensii Butterflyfish (Chaetodon mertensii) is a Pacific reef species belonging to a group sometimes called the 'crowned' or pearlyscale butterflyfishes, recognized by the distinctive scale patterning that gives the body a crosshatched appearance. It is generally regarded as one of the more manageable Chaetodon species in captivity, though it still requires a well-established system and attentive feeding during acclimation.

Reef compatibility: aquarium trade and reference sources consistently regard this species as not reef-safe. It has been documented nibbling on tubeworms, clams, large- and small-polyp stony corals, soft corals, and anemones. A fish-only or FOWLR (fish only with live rock) setup is generally advised. Experienced keepers occasionally report mixed success in reef systems, but this is the exception rather than the rule.

This species tends to do best as the sole specimen of its kind in the aquarium, unless tank volume and aquascape allow the simultaneous introduction of multiple peaceful butterflyfishes.
